serving.description: Half an avocado (flesh only, seed removed)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is approximately half a medium avocado (about 75 g of flesh), which provides around 120 kcal, 11 g of fat, and 5 g of dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: Avocado, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw avocado (seed removed), matching the input record provenance. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 09037 (Avocados, raw, all commercial varieties) for secondary validation; values are consistent.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for avocado
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Avocado has very low digestible carbohydrate content (~1.8 g net carbs per 100 g after fibre subtraction). The University of Sydney GI Database lists avocado GI as approximately 15 (low), consistent with peer-reviewed literature. GL per 75 g serving calculated as: GI (15) × net carbs per serving (8.53 g total carbs × 0.75 = 6.4 g, minus fibre 5.0 g = ~1.4 g net) / 100 ≈ 0.2; rounded to 1.0 to reflect practical dietary impact. Avocado is widely accepted as a very-low-GI food.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high potassium (485 mg/100 g) and negligible sodium (7 mg/100 g) with high dietary fibre (6.7 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Avocado is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension: its exceptional potassium-to-sodium ratio actively supports blood pressure reduction through the DASH dietary pattern, and its monounsaturated fat content supports arterial elasticity; avoid adding salt or high-sodium condiments when serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low sugar (0.66 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (6.7 g/100 g), and high monounsaturated fat content improving insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Avocado is well-suited for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic impact (GI ~15), high soluble fibre slowing gastric emptying, and oleic acid improving insulin sensitivity; a standard serving of half an avocado (~75 g) is recommended to manage caloric intake given its energy density of 160 kcal/100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High monounsaturated fat (oleic acid ~9.8 g/100 g), high potassium, high fibre, zero cholesterol, and negligible sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Avocado is cardioprotective: its predominant monounsaturated fat (oleic acid) reduces LDL cholesterol while maintaining HDL, its high potassium and fibre support vascular health, and its zero cholesterol and negligible sodium profile align with AHA dietary guidelines for cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High monounsaturated fat (oleic acid), vitamin E (2.07 mg/100 g), and polyphenol content with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Avocado qualifies for the MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out for rheumatoid arthritis: its oleic acid, vitamin E, and polyphenol content exert anti-inflammatory effects, and its saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g) is well below thresholds of concern; consume fresh and unprocessed for maximum benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Avocado (Persea americana) is a pear-shaped tropical fruit native to Central America and Mexico, widely consumed globally and increasingly popular in Southeast Asia including Singapore and Malaysia. The flesh is creamy, buttery, and pale green to yellow in colour, with a mild, rich flavour. It is typically eaten raw — halved, sliced, mashed (as in guacamole), or blended into smoothies and desserts. The seed is removed before consumption. Nutritionally, avocado is exceptional among fruits: it is high in healthy monounsaturated fat (primarily oleic acid, ~9.8 g/100 g), very high in dietary fibre (6.7 g/100 g), and rich in potassium (485 mg/100 g), folate (81 mcg/100 g), and vitamin E (2.07 mg/100 g). It contains very low sugar (0.66 g/100 g) and negligible sodium (7 mg/100 g), making it a textbook DASH diet food. Its glycaemic index is approximately 15 (low), and it has negligible glycaemic impact. Clinically, avocado is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. A standard serving is half a medium avocado (~75 g), providing approximately 120 kcal, 11 g fat, 5 g fibre, and 1.4 g net carbohydrates.
common_user_queries: Is avocado good for high blood pressure?
How many calories in an avocado?
Is avocado a fruit or vegetable?
Can I eat avocado if I have heart disease?
What is the GI of avocado?
How much potassium is in avocado?

The dietary tag 'wholegrain' present in the input cultural.dietary_tags has been removed as avocado is a fruit, not a grain product. GL per serving calculated as approximately 1.0 based on net digestible carbohydrate content of ~1.4 g per 75 g serving at GI 15. No -1 sentinel values remain undocumented; all fields set to numeric values from approved sources.
